sponsorOptions.name: Fiscal Sponsorship
sponsorOptions.shortDescription: 501(c)(3) nonprofit hosts your project under its tax-exempt umbrella

alternatives.name: NYFA (New York Foundation for the Arts)

pros: Can accept tax-deductible donations from day one
Can be the US petitioner for P-3 artist visa petitions
National legitimacy — grantors know and trust Fractured Atlas
cons: 8% fee on donations is not trivial at festival scale
Cannot access grants that explicitly exclude fiscally-sponsored projects
Dependent on sponsor for banking/accounting workflow
Not a long-term solution if building permanent infrastructure
sponsorOptions.model: Model C — Pre-Approved Grant Relationship (gives more independence)
sponsorOptions.recommendedFor: Year 1 pilot festival; combined with Indian focal sponsor and informal co-presenter partnerships

sponsorOptions.name: Partnership with Existing Indian-Arts 501(c)(3)
sponsorOptions.shortDescription: Co-present with established Indian-arts nonprofit; they are legal host, co-producer, and visa petitioner

fees.typical: 60/40 or 50/50 split of net revenue and shared risk

candidatePartners.name: Roundglass India Center
candidatePartners.credibility: CGI Seattle India Culture Week 2024 co-presenter

candidatePartners.name: The Music Circle
candidatePartners.credibility: Founded 1973 by Ravi Shankar; multiple ACTA grants

candidatePartners.name: Indian Fine Arts Academy of San Diego

pros: Co-presenter has venue, press, donor relationships
Strong signal to ICCR and consulate
cons: Lose creative control to some degree
Their existing programming calendar constrains yours
Finding the right partner is itself a 4-8 week process
sponsorOptions.rightFor: When institutional credibility matters more than creative control

sponsorOptions.name: Indian Focal Sponsor Only (US Partner TBD)
sponsorOptions.shortDescription: Indian individual, corporation, or foundation sponsors; US operational layer separate
sponsorOptions.fees: None directly; US-side operational structure adds fees
cannotDo: Apply for US grants requiring 501(c)(3) status
Petition P-3 artist visas (must be US entity)
canDo: Fund via Indian charitable trust if they have one
Provide marketing, artist relationships, production expertise from India
Give the festival institutional weight in India (helps ICCR applications)
pros: Fastest path if sponsor writes a big check
Simpler governance on US side if paired with fiscal sponsorship
cons: Still requires fiscal-sponsor or 501(c)(3)-partner for US-side operations
If structured as corporate payment vs grant, US tax treatment needs careful structuring
Legal clarity needed on whether Indian sponsor is a co-producer (rights/royalty questions)
sponsorOptions.p3VisaNote: P-3 visa petitioning requires US entity; pair this with Option A or B
sponsorOptions.rightFor: When you have a specific Indian sponsor with deep pockets and India-side relationships

recommendedCombination.rationale: Fractured Atlas gives US tax-exempt layer in 2-4 weeks. Indian focal sponsor provides India-side credibility and gap funding. An informal co-presenter relationship with a named Indian-arts 501(c)(3) (like Roundglass or The Music Circle) borrows institutional legitimacy without surrendering operational authority.

decisionChecklist: Does your Indian focal sponsor have a US affiliate or US bank relationship?
Will the sponsor's contribution be structured as a grant, sponsorship, or services?
Do you want the festival brand to be yours permanently, or is co-presenter branding acceptable?
What is the total projected budget? (Fractured Atlas's 8% fee looks different at $100K vs $500K)
Will you seek grants that explicitly require the applicant to be a direct 501(c)(3) and not a fiscally-sponsored project? (If yes, you need indian-501c3-partner, not fiscal-sponsor)
